title: Population by income level
subtitle: Total population, differentiated by World Bank income level.
caption:
Population by income level

Total population, differentiated by World Bank income level.

This stacked area chart tracks the total global population from 1960 to 2024, categorized by World Bank income levels. The vertical axis measures population in billions, ranging from 0 to 8 billion, while the horizontal axis marks time in ten-year increments. The data is visualized through four distinct colored bands stacked vertically. At the base, the pink band representing low-income countries remains the thinnest layer throughout the timeline, starting near zero in 1960 and expanding slightly to reach approximately 0.7 billion by 2024. Above this, the dark green band for lower-middle-income countries shows substantial growth, widening from roughly 1 billion in 1960 to nearly 3 billion in 2024, forming the largest single segment of the total population by the end of the period. The tan band for upper-middle-income countries sits above the green layer, also expanding significantly over the decades to contribute roughly 2.8 billion people by 2024. The top purple band, representing high-income countries, maintains a relatively consistent thickness compared to the lower bands, indicating slower proportional growth, though the absolute numbers increase slightly. The cumulative height of all bands rises steadily from approximately 3 billion in 1960 to just over 8 billion in 2024, illustrating continuous global population growth across all income categories, with the most visible expansion occurring in the lower-middle and upper-middle income segments.
